title: DCSync Credential Theft
briefing: Domain controller $TARGET logged Event ID 4662 showing $USER1 requesting the directory-replication rights DS-Replication-Get-Changes (GUID 1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2) and -Get-Changes-All. $USER1 is not a domain controller. This is DCSync: an attacker with delegated/stolen privileges is asking AD to replicate secrets, letting them pull password hashes (including krbtgt) for any account without touching a DC's disk. Confirm the credential-theft attempt and its blast radius.
dashboard_hint: Discover -> rule.id: 100138. Event ID 4662 where win.eventdata.properties contains the Replicating Directory Changes GUID (1131f6aa...). The red flag is that the requesting principal (subjectUserName) is a USER, not a DC machine account. Any non-DC principal replicating = DCSync.

options: DCSync — replicating directory secrets to steal password hashes
questions.answer: DCSync — replicating directory secrets to steal password hashes
questions.explain: A non-DC principal requesting DS-Replication-Get-Changes is DCSync: abusing AD replication to extract hashes, including krbtgt, for offline use.
The 4662 replication request lands on the domain controller $TARGET; the other hosts are unaffected.

questions.prompt: What makes this malicious rather than routine replication?
options: The requester is a user account, not a domain controller machine account
questions.answer: The requester is a user account, not a domain controller machine account
questions.explain: Only DCs legitimately replicate. A user (subjectUserName = $USER1) invoking Get-Changes-All is the DCSync signature.

questions.prompt: What is the worst-case impact if this succeeded?
options: Full domain compromise — attacker can dump krbtgt and forge Golden Tickets
A single file server is down
questions.answer: Full domain compromise — attacker can dump krbtgt and forge Golden Tickets
questions.explain: DCSync can pull the krbtgt hash, enabling Golden Tickets and effectively persistent domain-wide access.
DCSync (T1003.006) performed with valid, over-privileged credentials (T1078). No brute force or scan.

options: Rotate krbtgt password twice (with a wait between)
Reset $USER1 and audit who holds replication rights
Hunt for Golden Ticket use (anomalous TGT lifetimes)
Close the alert — replication is normal
answer: Rotate krbtgt password twice (with a wait between)
Reset $USER1 and audit who holds replication rights
Hunt for Golden Ticket use (anomalous TGT lifetimes)
questions.explain: Assume hashes (incl. krbtgt) are stolen: double-rotate krbtgt, reset the abusing account, strip unneeded replication rights, mass-reset privileged creds, and hunt for forged tickets.
